quick_facts.label: Core role
quick_facts.value: A Tehsildar (also spelled Tahsildar) heads the tehsil/taluka-level revenue office and carries revenue, executive and, in some states, quasi-judicial functions.

quick_facts.label: Land-records responsibility
quick_facts.value: The role commonly includes supervising land records, mutations and Record of Rights work, with authority and exact procedures determined under the relevant state’s land-revenue laws and rules.

quick_facts.label: Government-service entry
quick_facts.value: There is no single all-India recruitment route: state public-service commissions may recruit for Tehsildar/Tahsildar posts or upstream state civil services, while state revenue-service rules can also provide promotion routes into the cadre.

quick_facts.label: Field-facing administration
quick_facts.value: The job is not only desk-based: official descriptions include inspection/touring, supervision of village revenue staff, crop and boundary work, recovery of government dues, and local relief or emergency duties.

sections.heading: Responsibilities at tehsil level
paragraphs: A Tehsildar leads the local revenue administration for a tehsil or taluka. Core work typically includes collecting land revenue and other government dues; supervising the maintenance of village and tehsil land records; handling mutations and specified revenue cases; and overseeing subordinate revenue staff such as revenue inspectors, village accountants, patwaris or equivalent state-designated personnel.

The precise legal powers vary by state. Official descriptions show that Tehsildars may act in revenue or quasi-judicial matters, address land-record disputes and encroachments, issue or oversee certificates, and carry delegated executive-magisterial, disaster-relief or scheme-implementation duties where state law or district orders provide for them. Applicants should therefore read the applicable state land-revenue law, service rules and recruitment notification rather than assume identical powers across India.
bullets: Maintain oversight of land records, mutations, crop or record-of-rights registers, and revenue accounts.
Collect land revenue, fees, fines and other recoverable government dues as assigned under state law.
Hear or process revenue matters within delegated statutory authority, including specified land-record and mutation matters.
Supervise field revenue staff and conduct inspections/tours within the jurisdiction.
Support local administration during relief, emergency and implementation work when assigned.

sections.heading: Entry routes and how to verify eligibility
paragraphs: The route is state-specific, not a single national examination. A candidate may enter through a State PSC recruitment or state civil-services process and later receive a revenue posting; in some states, the commission also advertises Tehsildar/Tahsildar appointments directly. For example, the Kerala Public Service Commission has listed a Land Revenue recruitment for “Tahsildar/Senior Superintendent,” while Maharashtra’s PSC states that it conducts examinations for appointments to state-government services.

A second common route is departmental advancement through the revenue hierarchy. Rajasthan’s service rules expressly list promotion of Naib-Tehsildars among sources for promotion to Tehsildar. The operative eligibility—education, age, domicile or language conditions, reservation, papers, physical requirements if any, experience, and selection stages—must be taken from the current notification and state service rules for the particular vacancy.
bullets: Track the relevant State PSC and state Revenue/Board of Revenue websites; do not rely on coaching-site summaries for eligibility or deadlines.
For PSC civil-services routes, prepare for the commission’s notified preliminary, mains and interview/personality-test structure, where applicable.
For departmental routes, confirm feeder cadre, minimum service, required departmental examinations and promotion quota in the state rules.
Treat a notification’s post title carefully: “Tehsildar,” “Tahsildar,” “Deputy Tahsildar,” “Naib Tehsildar,” and state administrative-service postings are not interchangeable.

sections.heading: Skills that the role demands
paragraphs: The work calls for careful application of land-revenue procedures, strong record scrutiny and reasoned decision-making. Because the office supervises records, mutations, recoveries and revenue cases, an effective Tehsildar needs to interpret applicable rules, assess documentary evidence, maintain reliable case and record workflows, and communicate decisions clearly.

The position also requires field administration and people management. Official role descriptions refer to tours, inspection of revenue work, coordination with field personnel, contact with cultivators and local relief functions. These duties make public-facing communication, conflict handling, team supervision, integrity and the ability to work under time-sensitive administrative conditions especially important.
bullets: Working knowledge of the state’s land-revenue, land-record, registration, certificate and disaster-relief procedures relevant to the posting.
Document review, factual inquiry, record management and clear order/drafting skills.
Supervision of field staff and disciplined follow-up on inspections, registers and recoveries.
Public communication and fair handling of citizen grievances and land-related disputes.
Digital land-record and office-workflow capability as required by the state’s systems.

sections.heading: Workplace and operating environment
paragraphs: A Tehsildar is ordinarily based in a tehsil/taluka revenue office within the district administration, working below senior district or sub-divisional revenue authorities and alongside a field revenue network. The role combines office work—records, certificates, cases, correspondence and supervision—with travel inside the jurisdiction for inspections, local enquiries and coordination.

The workload can expand during land-record drives, revenue recovery cycles, elections or other assigned district duties, natural calamities and relief operations. The exact mix of executive, magisterial and quasi-judicial work differs by state and by delegation of powers.
bullets: Primary setting: tehsil/taluka office in the state Revenue Department or district administration.
Regular interaction: sub-divisional and district officers, revenue inspectors, village-level land-record staff, registries and members of the public.
Work pattern: a combination of case/record administration, public dealing, staff supervision and field inspections.

sections.heading: Progression context
paragraphs: Progression is governed by the relevant state cadre rules, vacancies and promotion procedures. A common revenue-cadre pattern is advancement from Naib-Tehsildar to Tehsildar; Rajasthan’s rules expressly identify Naib-Tehsildar promotion as a source for Tehsildar appointments. Himachal Pradesh PSC’s 2022–23 annual report also records consideration of eligible Naib-Tehsildars for promotion to Tehsildar, Class I (Gazetted), subject to stated service and departmental-examination conditions.

Further advancement is state-specific. An official Madhya Pradesh High Court judgment describes one officer’s historical career path from Naib-Tehsildar to Tehsildar and then Deputy Collector; this is an example of a state-specific trajectory, not a universal promotion guarantee.
bullets: Typical feeder role in several state systems: Naib-Tehsildar or an equivalent revenue-cadre post.
Promotion may depend on seniority/merit rules, minimum service, departmental examinations, vacancies, reservation provisions and departmental selection procedures.
Possible higher postings vary by state and may include senior revenue, sub-divisional or district-administration roles.
